request.question: What is the fourth episode in the American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon titled "Community"?

title: Rick and Morty
HarmonQuest
The Sarah Silverman Program
Harmontown
Great Minds with Dan Harmon
Acceptable.TV
Basic Rocket Science
Community (TV series)
List of Community characters
Introduction to Finality
sentences: Rick and Morty is an American adult animated science-fiction sitcom created by Justin Roiland and Dan Harmon for Cartoon Network's late-night programming block Adult Swim.

The series follows the misadventures of cynical mad scientist Rick Sanchez and his fretful, easily influenced grandson Morty Smith, who split their time between domestic family life and interdimensional adventures.
Roiland voices the series' eponymous characters, with the voice talent of Chris Parnell, Spencer Grammer, and Sarah Chalke providing the rest of the family.
It premiered on December 2, 2013.
HarmonQuest is an American adult animation/live improvisation television show created by Dan Harmon and Spencer Crittenden.
The show is part animated, part live action.
Dan Harmon and comedians Erin McGathy, Jeff B. Davis, along with game master Spencer Crittenden, perform a fantasy roleplaying campaign in front of a live audience at Victory Studios in Glendale, CA.
Each episode of the show also features a special guest player.

The content of their stories is then animated by Starburns Industries, and the episodes feature a mix of live and animated footage reminiscent of "The Ricky Gervais Show", which Harmon cited as an influence during a Facebook Live tour of Starburns Industries.

The Sarah Silverman Program is an American television sitcom, which ran from February 1, 2007 to April 15, 2010 on Comedy Central starring comedian and actress Sarah Silverman, who created the series with Dan Harmon and Rob Schrab.
Harmontown is a weekly live comedy podcast that began airing on June 6, 2012.
It is hosted by writer Dan Harmon, best known as the creator of the sitcom "Community", and actor Jeff B. Davis, best known for his work on the improvisational comedy series "Whose Line Is It Anyway?
" "Harmontown" began as a monthly live comedy show in Los Angeles, California at the NerdMelt Showroom on May 23, 2011, but the show became weekly after Harmon was fired from "Community".
Since the summer of 2012, each episode of "Harmontown" has been recorded and released as a podcast by Feral Audio.
Great Minds with Dan Harmon is an American comedy television series that premiered on February 25, 2016 on the History channel.

The series stars writer Dan Harmon (creator of "Community" and co-creator of "Rick and Morty") and his assistant Spencer Crittenden as themselves, who transport a series of historical figures to the present.
The series is part of History's "Night Class" programming block and was available to stream on History's website and YouTube channel.
The videos have since been removed but short clips are still available on YouTube.
Acceptable.TV is a television program created by Dan Harmon and Rob Schrab that aired on VH1 from March 23 to May 11, 2007.
Each show was composed of several mini-episodes created by the Acceptable.TV staff, with one mini-episode that was submitted by a viewer.
After each episode viewers would be able to vote online for their two favorites.
The two that received the most votes will be continued in the following episode, and the remaining three would be cancelled and replaced by new mini-shows.
The show was adapted from Harmon and Schrab's Channel 101 screenings, and the cast was composed of various Channel 101 contributors.
"Basic Rocket Science" is the fourth episode of the second season of "Community".
It was originally broadcast on October 14, 2010, on NBC.
Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon that aired on NBC and Yahoo! Screen from September 17, 2009 to June 2, 2015.

The series follows an ensemble cast of characters played by Joel McHale, Gillian Jacobs, Danny Pudi, Yvette Nicole Brown, Alison Brie, Donald Glover, Ken Jeong, Chevy Chase, and Jim Rash at a community college in the fictional town of Greendale, Colorado.
It makes heavy use of meta-humor and pop culture references, often parodying film and television clichés and tropes.
Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon.
The show, set at the fictional Greendale Community College, depicts the on-campus exploits of students brought together when they form a study group.
The show stars:
"Introduction to Finality" is the 22nd episode of the third season of the American television series "Community" and the third season finale.
It originally aired on May 17, 2012 on NBC.
This was the last episode to air with series creator Dan Harmon as showrunner before he was fired, though Harmon would later return as showrunner for the 5th season.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: What is the fourth episode in the American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon titled "Community"?
hop1_docs: List of Community characters | Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon.  The show, set at the fictional Greendale Community College, depicts the on-campus exploits of students brought together when they form a study group.  The show stars:

Emotional Consequences of Broadcast Television | "Emotional Consequences of Broadcast Television" is the series finale of the sitcom "Community", serving as the thirteenth episode of its sixth season.  It was written by series creator Dan Harmon and Chris McKenna, and directed by Rob Schrab.  It is the 110th episode overall and was first released on Yahoo! Screen in the United States on June 2, 2015.

Dan Harmon | Daniel Harmon (born January 3, 1973) is an American writer, producer, and voice actor.  Harmon created and produced the NBC comedy television series "Community", co-created the Adult Swim animated series "Rick and Morty", and co-founded the alternative television network/website Channel 101.  Harmon published "You'll Be Perfect When You're Dead" in 2013.  He also hosts a weekly eponymous podcast, "Harmontown."

Community (TV series) | Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon that aired on NBC and Yahoo! Screen from September 17, 2009 to June 2, 2015.  The series follows an ensemble cast of characters played by Joel McHale, Gillian Jacobs, Danny Pudi, Yvette Nicole Brown, Alison Brie, Donald Glover, Ken Jeong, Chevy Chase, and Jim Rash at a community college in the fictional town of Greendale, Colorado.  It makes heavy use of meta-humor and pop culture references, often parodying film and television clichés and tropes.

Introduction to Finality | "Introduction to Finality" is the 22nd episode of the third season of the American television series "Community" and the third season finale.  It originally aired on May 17, 2012 on NBC.  This was the last episode to air with series creator Dan Harmon as showrunner before he was fired, though Harmon would later return as showrunner for the 5th season.

The Sarah Silverman Program | The Sarah Silverman Program is an American television sitcom, which ran from February 1, 2007 to April 15, 2010 on Comedy Central starring comedian and actress Sarah Silverman, who created the series with Dan Harmon and Rob Schrab.

HarmonQuest | HarmonQuest is an American adult animation/live improvisation television show created by Dan Harmon and Spencer Crittenden.  The show is part animated, part live action.  Dan Harmon and comedians Erin McGathy, Jeff B. Davis, along with game master Spencer Crittenden, perform a fantasy roleplaying campaign in front of a live audience at Victory Studios in Glendale, CA.  Each episode of the show also features a special guest player.  The content of their stories is then animated by Starburns Industries, and the episodes feature a mix of live and animated footage reminiscent of "The Ricky Gervais Show", which Harmon cited as an influence during a Facebook Live tour of Starburns Industries.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Community — [1], [4]: "Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon."
- "Emotional Consequences of Broadcast Television" — [2]: "\"Emotional Consequences of Broadcast Television\""
- "110th episode overall" — [2]: "110th episode overall"
- "June 2, 2015" — [2]: "June 2, 2015"
- Dan Harmon / Daniel Harmon — [3]: "Daniel Harmon (born January 3, 1973)"
- "Greendale Community College" — [1]: "Greendale Community College"
- "Introduction to Finality" — [5]: "\"Introduction to Finality\""
- "22nd episode" — [5]: "22nd episode"
- "May 17, 2012" — [5]: "May 17, 2012"
- "September 17, 2009" — [4]: "September 17, 2009"
- "aired on NBC and Yahoo! Screen" — [4]: "aired on NBC and Yahoo! Screen"

Ambiguities:
- "fourth episode" could mean: fourth episode overall (by series airing order) OR fourth episode of a particular season (e.g., season 1 episode 4). Passages do not disambiguate which interpretation is intended.
- No episode titled as "fourth" is present; multiple episode titles are present for other positions ("Introduction to Finality", "Emotional Consequences of Broadcast Television"), so episode numbering vs title mapping is unresolved.

Bridge / missing fact:
- Missing: the title of the fourth episode of Community (the episode occupying episode number 4 in the series' airing order), and clarification of whether "fourth episode" refers to series-wide order or season-specific order.

Retrieval direction:
1. Open page "List of Community episodes" (Wikipedia) to get episode #4 by airing order.
2. Open page "Community (season 1)" (Wikipedia) to confirm season 1 episode 4 and its title.
3. Search query: "Community episode 4 title" (or exact query: "Community episode 4 'title'") if a direct page lookup fails.
4. If needed, open the episode page matching candidate title (e.g., "Social Psychology (Community)") to confirm it is episode 4.

Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
hop2_docs: Revelations (Battlestar Galactica) | "Revelations" is the twelfth episode in the fourth season of the reimagined "Battlestar Galactica".  It first aired on television in the United States on June 13, 2008.  The episode serves as the mid-season finale of the fourth season, with the concluding episodes of the series airing after a hiatus.  The survivor count shown in the title sequence is 39,665.

History 101 (Community) | "History 101" is the first episode of the fourth season of "Community", which originally aired on February 7, 2013 on NBC.  The episode was written by Andy Bobrow and directed by Tristram Shapeero.  The episode makes heavy use of a parody focusing on "The Hunger Games".  This episode marks the first in the series to have no involvement from series creator Dan Harmon.

The Narrow Escape Problem | "The Narrow Escape Problem" is the fourth episode of the third season of the FX anthology series "Fargo", and the twenty-fourth episode of the series overall.  It was directed by Michael Uppendahl, and written by series executive producer Monica Beletsky.  The title refers to the eponymous problem in biology, biophysics and cellular biology.

Orientation (Heroes) | "Orientation" is the first episode of the fourth season of the NBC science fiction drama series "Heroes" and the 60th episode overall.  The episode aired in the US on September 21, 2009.  Produced and filmed as two separate episodes, the episode's title for the second hour was initially announced as "Jump, Push, Fall"; however, it aired as a single double-length episode.

The First Stone (Rebus) | The First Stone is a 2007 episode of STV's "Rebus" television series.  It was the second episode broadcast in the show's fourth season, and starred Ken Stott in the title role.  The episode was based on an Ian Rankin short story.

Resurrection Men (Rebus) | Resurrection Men is a 2007 episode of STV's "Rebus" television series.  It was the first episode broadcast in the show's fourth season, and starred Ken Stott in the title role.  The episode was based on the Ian Rankin novel of the same name.

The Hot Troll Deviation | "The Hot Troll Deviation" is the fourth episode of the fourth season of the US sitcom "The Big Bang Theory" and the 67th episode of the show overall.  It first aired on CBS on October 14, 2010.  In the episode, Howard and Bernadette go on a date following their breakup a few months prior.  Sheldon and Raj engage in a series of "tit for tat" exchanges when working together.  The title refers to Howard's virtual affair with a troll on World of Warcraft.
response.summary_2: No supporting evidence in the provided passages naming the fourth episode of Community.
